data.title: Cadillac Mines Prices, Upsizes Initial Public Offering
data.description:
05:40 AM EDT, 07/24/2026 (MT Newswires) -- Cadillac Mines has priced and upsized its initial public offering to raise gross proceeds of about C$385 million, the company said Thursday.

Cadillac Mines priced 47.1 million common shares at C$6.90 per share and 6.3 million special flow-through shares at C$9.52 per share. The offering was upsized from about C$363 million, the company said, citing excess demand.

Underwriters were granted an over-allotment option to buy up to an additional 8.0 million common shares at the offering price. Agnico Eagle Mines ( AEM ) has also subscribed for 8.7 million common shares on a private placement basis for gross proceeds of about C$60 million.

The offering is scheduled to close on or about Aug. 5. The common shares will start trading on the Toronto Stock Exchange on July 24 under the symbol CADY.

MT Newswires does not provide investment advice. Unauthorized reproduction is strictly prohibited.

data.title: Burford Capital Counterparty Wins $600 Million in Arbitration Ruling
data.description:
05:18 AM EDT, 07/24/2026 (MT Newswires) -- Burford Capital ( BUR ) said Friday that an ICC International Court of Arbitration tribunal awarded over $600 million to one of its counterparties in a Cameroon mining arbitration matter.

The company said that if the award were paid in full immediately, its entitlement would exceed 250 million Australian dollars ($174.7 million), with the entire return accruing to Burford's balance sheet and none to its investment funds.

Burford said that the award does not represent cash proceeds received and remains subject to significant litigation and collection risks.

The company said it could end up getting far less than this amount or even losing its entire investment depending on future court outcomes.

Shares of the company were up 1.2% in Friday premarket activity.

MT Newswires does not provide investment advice. Unauthorized reproduction is strictly prohibited.

data.title: Manitowoc Backs US Ruling on Dumped Japanese Crawler Cranes
data.description:
05:00 AM EDT, 07/24/2026 (MT Newswires) -- Manitowoc (MTW) said Thursday it welcomed a unanimous US International Trade Commission and US Department of Commerce decision finding that Japanese producers of lattice-boom crawler cranes engaged in predatory dumping.

The company said the ruling supports fair competition and its US manufacturing operations and workforce.

The new antidumping duties are scheduled to take effect this month, it added.

data.title: Oracle Wins Up to $7 Billion US Department of War Software Contract
data.description:
05:01 AM EDT, 07/24/2026 (MT Newswires) -- Oracle (ORCL) won an enterprise software agreement from the US Department of War with a potential value of up to $7 billion over a 10-year term, the department said Thursday.

The agreement, negotiated by the Department of the Navy, has a five-year base period and a five-year option period. The Department of War said the contract consolidates software procurement under a single enterprise agreement to replace fragmented purchasing across the department.

The department said the agreement is projected to save at least $441 million over its lifecycle through consolidated procurement and standardized software acquisition.

Financial terms beyond the contract ceiling were not disclosed.

data.title: Top apparel exporter Vietnam faces higher US tariffs than peers
data.description:
HANOI, July 24 (Reuters) - Vietnam faces higher U.S. tariffs than peers and risks a further erosion of competitiveness in the clothing sector, as the largest exporter of apparel to the U.S. is excluded from a mechanism that could cut U.S. duties on some textile imports.

The decision, included in a Federal Register notice published on Thursday, could impact the supply chains of major apparel brands with large manufacturing bases in Vietnam.

Among top investors in Vietnam are Nike ( NKE ), Gap, Ralph Lauren ( RL ) and Under Armour ( UAA ). The companies did not respond immediately to requests for comment.

The new tariffs of 10% and 12.5%, which take effect on Friday, target 60 trading partners over allegations of lax enforcement of forced labour bans.

Vietnam, which is still negotiating a trade deal with the U.S. under the Trump administration, faces a 12.5% tariff rate, as does China. Competitors Bangladesh, Cambodia, Indonesia and Malaysia, which have done trade deals, are subject to 10% duties.

The measures are intended to replace temporary 10% tariffs expiring on Friday that the Trump administration imposed after the U.S. Supreme Court in February struck down its "reciprocal" duties of 10% to 50% that had been introduced under emergency powers.

Vietnam overtook China last year as the largest exporter of apparel to the U.S., and it is also one of the countries with the largest trade surpluses with Washington, according to U.S. trade data.

Vietnam's large garment trade is now exposed to a double disadvantage.

Bangladesh, Cambodia, Indonesia and Malaysia are also the only beneficiaries named in the notice of a new "textile mechanism" that could exempt part of their apparel exports to the U.S. from the new tariffs.

The mechanism, to be established "when feasible" and initially set to last three years, is designed to encourage imports of U.S. cotton and textile goods.

A portion of eligible apparel exports from these countries would qualify for reduced or zero additional tariffs under a quota system linked to their purchases of U.S. cotton and textile inputs, according to the notice.

Vietnam's foreign and trade ministries did not immediately respond to requests for comment.

(Reporting by Francesco Guarascio and Khanh Vu; Editing by Jamie Freed)
